why: why is my removed element still in memory
how: how to delete html element with javascript
output: failed to execute removeChild on node parameter is not of type node

1_beginner_explanation.summary: To delete an element from the page, you select it and call the `.remove()` method. It instantly disappears from the screen and the DOM tree. If you want to empty out a container (like deleting all items inside a list), you can set its `innerHTML` to an empty string `''`.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Calling `.remove()` is like taking a pair of scissors and cutting a leaf off the DOM tree. It falls to the ground and is swept up by the Garbage Collector.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When `.remove()` is called, the browser breaks the pointer links connecting the node to its parent and siblings. It then triggers a layout recalculation (Reflow) so the surrounding elements shift into the empty space.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Removing a node from the DOM does NOT instantly destroy it in memory! If you have a JavaScript variable holding a reference to that node (e.g., `const myBox = document.querySelector('.box')`), the object will stay in the Heap forever (Memory Leak). You must set `myBox = null` to let the Garbage Collector destroy it.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Deleting a notification banner when the user clicks 'Dismiss'.

4_common_confusion.trap: Trying to remove an element that isn't on the page, or trying to use `removeChild` incorrectly by passing an ID string instead of a Node object. `parent.removeChild('myId')` will throw an error.
4_common_confusion.fix: Always use the modern `element.remove()` method. It is much simpler and less prone to errors.

6_edge_case.quirk: If you remove an element that has event listeners attached to it, modern browsers are smart enough to garbage collect the event listeners too (preventing memory leaks), AS LONG AS you don't have lingering JS variables pointing to the element.
